title_en: Sahih, Hasan, and Da'if: The Core Quality Divisions
title_ar: الصحيح والحسن والضعيف: التقسيمات الجوهرية للجودة
content_en:
The central classification in Nukhbat al-Fikr for the practical purposes of hadith evaluation is the three-way division of ahad hadiths into sahih (sound), hasan (good), and da'if (weak). Ibn Hajar presents these with greater precision and logical clarity than most earlier works, and his definitions have become the standard reference points for contemporary hadith methodology.

The Sahih Hadith

Ibn Hajar defines the sahih hadith as one whose chain of transmission is connected (meaning each narrator directly heard from the one above him), all of whose narrators are morally upright and precise in retention, and which is free from both hidden defects (illa) and irregularity (shudhudh). This five-condition definition follows the earlier tradition but is stated with particular clarity.

He further distinguishes between sahih li dhatihi (intrinsically sound) — a hadith that meets all conditions on its own — and sahih li ghayrihi (sound by virtue of others) — a hasan hadith that is elevated to the rank of sahih because of supporting transmissions from other chains. This distinction acknowledges that a single hasan chain can be strengthened into the sahih category when multiple independent chains transmit the same content.

The question of whether a sahih hadith generates certain knowledge or merely probable knowledge divided earlier scholars. Ibn Hajar takes the view that the conditions of sahih are designed to maximize probability to the point where rational doubt is unreasonable, but that theoretical fallibility remains — only the mutawatir generates absolute certainty. This epistemological humility is important: it means that even scholars who accept sahih hadiths as legal and theological proofs acknowledge that new evidence could in principle require reassessment.

The Hasan Hadith

The hasan (good) category is the most distinctively developed by Ibn Hajar. Earlier scholars had struggled to define hasan precisely — al-Tirmidhi's famous definition ('its chain is not isolated with a single narrator known as a liar, and is not irregular, and it is narrated from more than one direction') was widely used but considered imprecise. Ibn Hajar's definition focuses on the precision (dabt) criterion: a hasan hadith is one that meets all the conditions of sahih except that one or more narrators in the chain, while morally sound, are slightly below the highest grade of precision.

The Da'if Hadith and Its Varieties

Ibn Hajar identifies two types of weakness that produce da'if hadiths. The first type involves defects in the chain's continuity: the mursal (where a Successor cites the Prophet without naming the Companion intermediary), the munqati' (where a named narrator is omitted), the mudallis (where a narrator implies direct transmission from a teacher he never met), and several other technical types. The second type involves defects in the narrators' qualities: the presence of someone unknown (majhul), someone accused of lying (muttaham bi al-kadhib), or someone with a history of error (kathir al-khata').

Each defect type has a name and specific implications for how the hadith should be treated. Ibn Hajar's logical taxonomy of defect types — presented as a branching tree structure parallel to the quality classifications — gives Nukhbat al-Fikr much of its pedagogical value. Students who mastered the taxonomy could identify the specific weakening factor in any given hadith and understand its implications for the hadith's usability.
